notes.heading: Introduction to Biomolecules
notes.text: Biomolecules are naturally occurring chemical substances present in living organisms. They are essential for life processes such as growth, energy production, repair, protection, reproduction, heredity and regulation of body functions.
points: Examples of biomolecules include carbohydrates, proteins, vitamins, nucleic acids and hormones.
Carbohydrates mainly provide energy. Examples: glucose, sucrose, starch and cellulose.
Proteins help in body building, repair, enzyme action, hormone formation and transport. Examples: insulin, haemoglobin and enzymes.
Vitamins are required in small amounts for proper body functioning. Examples: vitamins A, B, C, D, E and K.
Nucleic acids control heredity and protein synthesis. Examples: DNA and RNA.
Biomolecules are mostly made of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur and phosphorus.
Biomolecules are mainly studied under organic chemistry because most of them are carbon compounds.
Simple definition: Biomolecules are naturally occurring chemical substances present in living organisms which are essential for life processes.

notes.heading: Carbohydrates: Introduction and Classification
notes.text: Carbohydrates are biomolecules mainly made up of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen. They are commonly called sugars or hydrates of carbon.
points: The general formula of many carbohydrates is written as Cx(H2O)y.
Example: Glucose has the formula C6H12O6 and may be written as C6(H2O)6.
All carbohydrates do not exactly follow the hydrate of carbon formula.
NCERT definition: Carbohydrates are optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, or compounds which give such units on hydrolysis.
Polyhydroxy means having many -OH groups.
An aldehyde contains the -CHO group.
A ketone contains a C=O group inside the carbon chain.
Glucose contains an aldehyde group, so it is an aldose.
Fructose contains a ketone group, so it is a ketose.
Carbohydrates are classified based on hydrolysis into monosaccharides, oligosaccharides and polysaccharides.
Monosaccharides are the simplest carbohydrates and cannot be hydrolysed into smaller carbohydrates. Examples: glucose, fructose, ribose and galactose.
Glucose and fructose have the same molecular formula, C6H12O6, but different structures.
Oligosaccharides give 2 to 10 monosaccharide units on hydrolysis.
Disaccharides are oligosaccharides which give two monosaccharide units on hydrolysis.
Sucrose on hydrolysis gives glucose and fructose.
Maltose on hydrolysis gives glucose and glucose.
Lactose on hydrolysis gives glucose and galactose.
Polysaccharides give many monosaccharide units on hydrolysis and have high molecular mass.
Examples of polysaccharides are starch, cellulose and glycogen.
Polysaccharides are generally not sweet in taste.
Carbohydrates containing an aldehyde group are called aldoses. Example: glucose.
Carbohydrates containing a ketone group are called ketoses. Example: fructose.
Classification based on number of carbon atoms: triose has 3 carbon atoms, tetrose has 4, pentose has 5 and hexose has 6.
Glucose is an aldohexose because it contains an aldehyde group and has six carbon atoms.
Fructose is a ketohexose because it contains a ketone group and has six carbon atoms.

notes.heading: Glucose: Preparation and Open Chain Structure
notes.text: Glucose is the most important monosaccharide. It is also called dextrose because it is dextrorotatory, meaning it rotates plane-polarised light towards the right.
points: Molecular formula of glucose: C6H12O6.
Glucose is an aldohexose because it has six carbon atoms and contains an aldehyde group.
Preparation from sucrose: When sucrose is hydrolysed with dilute acid or enzyme, it gives glucose and fructose.
Reaction: C12H22O11 + H2O -> C6H12O6 + C6H12O6.
Sucrose -> Glucose + Fructose.
The mixture formed by hydrolysis of sucrose is called invert sugar.
Preparation from starch: Glucose is commercially prepared by hydrolysis of starch using dilute sulphuric acid.
Reaction: (C6H10O5)n + nH2O -> nC6H12O6.
Starch -> Glucose.
Evidence for the structure of glucose: When glucose is heated with HI and red phosphorus, it gives n-hexane. This proves that glucose has a straight chain of six carbon atoms.
Reaction: Glucose --HI/red P--> n-hexane.
Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine to form glucose oxime.
Reaction: Glucose + NH2OH -> Glucose oxime.
Glucose reacts with hydrogen cyanide to form glucose cyanohydrin.
Reaction: Glucose + HCN -> Glucose cyanohydrin.
The reactions with hydroxylamine and HCN show the presence of a carbonyl group.
Glucose is oxidised by bromine water to form gluconic acid.
Reaction: Glucose --Br2/H2O--> Gluconic acid.
Oxidation with bromine water proves that the carbonyl group in glucose is an aldehyde group.
Glucose reacts with acetic anhydride to form glucose pentaacetate. This proves that glucose contains five hydroxyl groups.
Reaction: Glucose + 5(CH3CO)2O -> Glucose pentaacetate.
Open chain structure of glucose: CHO-(CHOH)4-CH2OH.
Expanded structure: CHO-CHOH-CHOH-CHOH-CHOH-CH2OH.
IUPAC type description: 2,3,4,5,6-pentahydroxyhexanal.
Limitation of open chain structure: It explains many properties but cannot explain all reactions of glucose.
Glucose does not give some typical aldehyde reactions even though the open chain structure contains an aldehyde group.
Therefore, glucose exists mostly in cyclic form.

notes.heading: Cyclic Structure of Glucose and Anomers
notes.text: Glucose mainly exists in cyclic hemiacetal form because the free aldehyde group is not present in most glucose molecules.
points: Open chain structure of glucose: CHO-(CHOH)4-CH2OH.
The open chain structure cannot explain all properties of glucose.
Glucose does not react with sodium bisulphite, NaHSO3, which is a typical aldehyde reaction.
Glucose pentaacetate does not react with hydroxylamine.
These observations show that the aldehyde group is not freely present in glucose.
In glucose, the aldehyde group at carbon 1 reacts with the hydroxyl group at carbon 5.
This intramolecular reaction forms a six-membered ring.
The six-membered ring structure is called a pyranose structure because it resembles pyran.
When an aldehyde reacts with an alcohol, it forms a hemiacetal.
Glucose forms an intramolecular hemiacetal.
During cyclic structure formation, carbon 1 becomes a new asymmetric carbon.
Carbon 1 in glucose is called the anomeric carbon.
Due to formation of the new chiral carbon, glucose exists in two forms: alpha-D-glucose and beta-D-glucose.
In alpha-D-glucose, the -OH group on carbon 1 is on the opposite side to the CH2OH group.
For D-glucose, in the alpha form, the OH on carbon 1 is down and the CH2OH group is up.
In beta-D-glucose, the -OH group on carbon 1 is on the same side as the CH2OH group.
For D-glucose, in the beta form, the OH on carbon 1 is up and the CH2OH group is up.
Anomers are stereoisomers which differ in configuration only at the anomeric carbon.
Alpha-D-glucose and beta-D-glucose are anomers because they differ only at carbon 1.
The cyclic structure of glucose is represented by Haworth projection.
In Haworth projection of D-glucose, the ring contains five carbon atoms and one oxygen atom.
The ring is six-membered.
The CH2OH group is placed above the ring for D-glucose.
Alpha and beta forms differ in the position of -OH on carbon 1.
Mutarotation is the change in optical rotation when alpha-D-glucose or beta-D-glucose is dissolved in water and gradually reaches an equilibrium value.
Mutarotation happens because alpha and beta forms change into each other through the open chain form.
Equilibrium: alpha-D-glucose <-> open chain form <-> beta-D-glucose.

notes.heading: Fructose: Structure and Cyclic Form
notes.text: Fructose is an important monosaccharide also called fruit sugar because it is commonly found in fruits and honey.
points: Molecular formula of fructose: C6H12O6.
Fructose and glucose have the same molecular formula but different structures, so they are functional isomers.
Fructose is called a ketohexose because it contains six carbon atoms and a ketone group.
Open chain structure of fructose: CH2OH-CO-(CHOH)3-CH2OH.
Expanded structure: CH2OH-CO-CHOH-CHOH-CHOH-CH2OH.
The ketone group in fructose is present at carbon 2.
IUPAC type description: 1,3,4,5,6-pentahydroxyhexan-2-one.
Like glucose, fructose mainly exists in cyclic form.
In fructose, the ketone group at carbon 2 reacts with the -OH group of carbon 5.
This forms a five-membered ring.
The five-membered cyclic structure is called a furanose structure because it resembles furan.
In fructose, carbon 2 becomes the new chiral carbon after ring formation.
Carbon 2 in fructose is the anomeric carbon.
Fructose exists in two anomeric forms: alpha-D-fructose and beta-D-fructose.
In alpha-D-fructose, the -OH group at the anomeric carbon is on the opposite side to the CH2OH group.
In beta-D-fructose, the -OH group at the anomeric carbon is on the same side as the CH2OH group.
Fructose mainly exists as a five-membered cyclic structure called fructofuranose.
Difference between glucose and fructose: glucose is an aldohexose and fructose is a ketohexose.
Glucose contains an aldehyde group, while fructose contains a ketone group.
The carbonyl carbon is C1 in glucose and C2 in fructose.
Glucose forms a six-membered glucopyranose ring, while fructose forms a five-membered fructofuranose ring.
The anomeric carbon is C1 in glucose and C2 in fructose.

notes.heading: Disaccharides: Sucrose, Maltose and Lactose
notes.text: Disaccharides are carbohydrates which give two monosaccharide units on hydrolysis.
points: General reaction: Disaccharide + H2O -> Monosaccharide + Monosaccharide.
Important examples of disaccharides are sucrose, maltose and lactose.
Sucrose is commonly known as table sugar.
Sucrose is obtained from sugarcane and beetroot.
Molecular formula of sucrose: C12H22O11.
On hydrolysis, sucrose gives glucose and fructose.
Reaction: C12H22O11 + H2O -> C6H12O6 + C6H12O6.
Sucrose -> Glucose + Fructose.
In sucrose, glucose and fructose are joined by a glycosidic linkage between C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose.
The linkage in sucrose is between alpha-D-glucose C1 and beta-D-fructose C2.
Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar because both anomeric carbons are involved in glycosidic linkage.
Anomeric carbon of glucose is C1 and anomeric carbon of fructose is C2.
Sucrose does not have a free aldehyde or ketone group.
Sucrose does not reduce Fehling's solution or Tollens' reagent.
Invert sugar is the mixture of glucose and fructose obtained by hydrolysis of sucrose.
Sucrose is dextrorotatory, but after hydrolysis the mixture becomes laevorotatory because fructose has stronger laevorotation.
Maltose is a disaccharide formed by joining two glucose units.
Maltose -> Glucose + Glucose.
In maltose, two glucose units are joined through C1 of one glucose and C4 of another glucose.
The linkage in maltose is alpha-1,4-glycosidic linkage.
Maltose is a reducing sugar because one anomeric carbon is free.
Maltose can reduce Fehling's solution and Tollens' reagent.
Lactose is commonly known as milk sugar and is found in milk.
On hydrolysis, lactose gives galactose and glucose.
Lactose -> Galactose + Glucose.
In lactose, galactose and glucose are joined by beta-1,4-glycosidic linkage.
The linkage in lactose is between C1 of galactose and C4 of glucose.
Lactose is a reducing sugar because one anomeric carbon is free.
Lactose can reduce Fehling's solution and Tollens' reagent.
Reducing sugars reduce Fehling's solution or Tollens' reagent. Examples: glucose, fructose, maltose and lactose.
Non-reducing sugars do not reduce Fehling's solution or Tollens' reagent. Example: sucrose.
Important comparison: sucrose gives glucose and fructose and is non-reducing; maltose gives glucose and glucose and is reducing; lactose gives glucose and galactose and is reducing.

notes.heading: Polysaccharides: Starch, Cellulose and Glycogen
notes.text: Polysaccharides are carbohydrates which give a large number of monosaccharide units on hydrolysis. They are also called complex carbohydrates.
points: General reaction: Polysaccharide + H2O -> Many monosaccharide units.
Important examples of polysaccharides are starch, cellulose and glycogen.
Polysaccharides are high molecular mass carbohydrates.
They are generally not sweet.
They are mostly insoluble in water.
They are made up of many monosaccharide units.
They are used for storage or structural support.
Starch is the main storage carbohydrate in plants.
Plants store glucose in the form of starch.
Starch is found in rice, wheat, potato and maize.
Starch is a polymer of alpha-D-glucose.
Starch contains two components: amylose and amylopectin.
Amylose is a linear polymer of glucose.
In amylose, glucose units are joined by alpha-1,4-glycosidic linkage.
Amylose is soluble in water.
Amylopectin is a branched polymer of glucose.
Amylopectin has alpha-1,4-glycosidic linkage in the main chain and alpha-1,6-glycosidic linkage at branching points.
Amylopectin is insoluble in water.
On hydrolysis, starch gives glucose.
Reaction: (C6H10O5)n + nH2O -> nC6H12O6.
Starch -> Glucose.
Cellulose is the chief structural material of plant cell walls.
Cellulose gives strength and support to plants.
Cellulose is found in cotton, wood and plant fibres.
Cellulose is a polymer of beta-D-glucose.
In cellulose, glucose units are joined by beta-1,4-glycosidic linkage.
Humans cannot digest cellulose because they do not have the enzyme needed to break beta-1,4-glycosidic linkage.
Animals like cows and buffaloes can digest cellulose with the help of microorganisms present in their stomach.
Glycogen is the storage carbohydrate in animals.
Glycogen is also called animal starch.
Glycogen is mainly stored in liver and muscles.
Glycogen is a highly branched polymer of alpha-D-glucose.
Glycogen is similar to amylopectin but more highly branched.
When the body needs energy, glycogen breaks down into glucose.
Glycogen -> Glucose.
Starch is found in plants and functions as a storage carbohydrate.
Cellulose is found in plant cell walls and functions as structural support.
Glycogen is found in animals and functions as a storage carbohydrate.

notes.heading: Proteins: Amino Acids and Peptide Bond
notes.text: Proteins are biomolecules made up of many amino acid units joined together. They are essential for growth, repair, enzyme formation, hormone formation, transport and body defence.
points: Examples of proteins include haemoglobin, insulin, enzymes, keratin and collagen.
Amino acids are the basic building blocks of proteins.
Amino acids contain an amino group, -NH2, and a carboxylic acid group, -COOH.
General structure of an amino acid: H2N-CH(R)-COOH.
R represents the side chain. Different amino acids have different R groups.
Glycine is the simplest amino acid.
Structure of glycine: H2N-CH2-COOH.
In glycine, the R group is hydrogen.
Alanine has a methyl group as side chain.
Structure of alanine: H2N-CH(CH3)-COOH.
Most naturally occurring amino acids are alpha-amino acids.
In alpha-amino acids, the amino group is attached to the carbon next to the carboxyl group.
The carbon next to the carboxyl group is called alpha carbon.
General structure of alpha-amino acids: H2N-CH(R)-COOH.
Essential amino acids cannot be synthesised by the body and must be supplied through diet.
Examples of essential amino acids: valine, leucine and lysine.
Non-essential amino acids can be synthesised by the body.
Examples of non-essential amino acids: glycine and alanine.
Amino acids contain both acidic and basic groups.
The carboxyl group can lose a proton: -COOH -> -COO-.
The amino group can accept a proton: -NH2 -> -NH3+.
Therefore, amino acids exist as zwitter ions.
General zwitter ion structure: +NH3-CH(R)-COO-.
A zwitter ion has both positive and negative charges but is overall neutral.
A peptide bond is formed when the carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another amino acid with loss of water.
Peptide bond reaction: -COOH + -NH2 -> -CO-NH- + H2O.
The peptide bond linkage is -CO-NH-.
Two amino acids join to form a dipeptide.
Reaction: Amino acid + Amino acid -> Dipeptide + H2O.
Many amino acids join by peptide bonds to form a polypeptide chain.
Proteins are made up of one or more polypeptide chains.
Dipeptide has 2 amino acid units.
Tripeptide has 3 amino acid units.
Polypeptide has many amino acid units.
A protein is a large polypeptide chain.
Proteins are polymers of alpha-amino acids.

notes.heading: Classification and Structure of Proteins
notes.text: Proteins are polymers of alpha-amino acids joined by peptide bonds. They are classified mainly on the basis of shape into fibrous proteins and globular proteins.
points: General idea: Amino acids --peptide bonds--> Protein.
Fibrous proteins have a long thread-like structure.
Fibrous proteins are generally insoluble in water.
Fibrous proteins mainly give strength and support.
Examples of fibrous proteins: keratin, collagen and myosin.
Keratin is found in hair, nails, wool and feathers.
Collagen is found in skin, bones and tendons.
Globular proteins have a spherical or ball-like shape.
Globular proteins are generally soluble in water.
Globular proteins mainly perform biological functions.
Examples of globular proteins: insulin, haemoglobin, albumin and enzymes.
Insulin controls blood sugar level.
Haemoglobin transports oxygen in blood.
Protein structure is studied at four levels: primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary.
Primary structure is the linear sequence of amino acids in a protein.
Example of sequence: Glycine-Alanine-Valine-Leucine.
The order of amino acids is very important because even a small change in sequence can change protein function.
Exam definition: Primary structure of a protein is the specific sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide chain.
Secondary structure refers to the regular folding of the polypeptide chain due to hydrogen bonding.
Important secondary structures are alpha-helix and beta-pleated sheet.
In alpha-helix, the polypeptide chain forms a spiral structure.
Alpha-helix is stabilised by hydrogen bonds.
Example of alpha-helix protein: alpha-keratin.
In beta-pleated sheet, polypeptide chains lie side by side like a folded sheet.
Beta-pleated sheet structure is also stabilised by hydrogen bonds.
Example of beta-pleated sheet protein: silk fibroin.
Tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional shape of a protein.
Tertiary structure forms due to folding of the secondary structure.
Tertiary structure is stabilised by hydrogen bonds, disulphide bonds, ionic bonds and van der Waals forces.
Tertiary structure gives the protein its biological activity.
Quaternary structure is the arrangement of multiple polypeptide chains in a protein.
Example: haemoglobin has four polypeptide chains.
Denaturation is the process in which a protein loses its natural structure due to heat, change in pH or chemicals.
During denaturation, secondary and tertiary structures are destroyed.
During denaturation, primary structure remains unchanged.
A denatured protein loses biological activity.
Examples of denaturation: boiling of egg and curdling of milk.

notes.heading: Enzymes
notes.text: Enzymes are biological catalysts. They increase the rate of biochemical reactions in living organisms without being consumed in the reaction.
points: Most enzymes are globular proteins.
Simple definition: Enzymes are proteins which catalyse biological reactions in living systems.
Digestion of food in the body takes place with the help of enzymes.
Amylase converts starch into maltose.
Maltase converts maltose into glucose.
Invertase converts sucrose into glucose and fructose.
Zymase converts glucose into ethanol and carbon dioxide.
Pepsin helps in digestion of proteins in the stomach.
Trypsin helps in digestion of proteins in the intestine.
Enzymes act on specific substances called substrates.
The enzyme combines with the substrate to form an enzyme-substrate complex.
Enzyme action: E + S -> ES.
The enzyme-substrate complex breaks down to form product and free enzyme.
Reaction: ES -> E + P.
Overall reaction: E + S -> ES -> E + P.
E means enzyme, S means substrate, ES means enzyme-substrate complex and P means product.
Example: Sucrose + H2O --Invertase--> Glucose + Fructose.
In the sucrose reaction, enzyme is invertase, substrate is sucrose, and products are glucose and fructose.
Enzymes are highly specific, meaning one enzyme generally acts on only one particular substrate.
Example: urease acts only on urea.
Reaction: NH2CONH2 + H2O --Urease--> 2NH3 + CO2.
The enzyme has a specific region called the active site.
In the lock and key model, the substrate fits into the active site like a key fits into a lock.
Enzyme is compared to a lock and substrate is compared to a key.
Only the correct substrate can fit into the active site.
Enzymes are highly efficient; a small amount of enzyme can catalyse a large amount of reaction.
Most enzymes work best around body temperature, about 37 degrees Celsius.
At high temperature, enzymes may get denatured.
Each enzyme has a particular pH at which it works best.
Pepsin works in acidic medium in the stomach.
Certain substances can reduce or stop enzyme activity. These are called enzyme inhibitors.
Since most enzymes are proteins, they can be denatured by high temperature, strong acids, strong bases and chemicals.
After denaturation, an enzyme loses its shape and activity.

notes.heading: Vitamins: Classification and Deficiency Diseases
notes.text: Vitamins are organic compounds required by the body in small amounts for proper growth and normal health.
points: Vitamins do not provide energy like carbohydrates, but they help in many important body functions.
Definition: Vitamins are organic substances which are required in small quantities in the diet for proper growth and maintenance of good health.
Vitamins help in body growth, protection from diseases, proper functioning of eyes, skin, bones and blood, metabolism and immunity.
Vitamins are classified into fat-soluble vitamins and water-soluble vitamins.
Fat-soluble vitamins dissolve in fats and oils and are stored in the body.
Fat-soluble vitamins are A, D, E and K.
Memory trick for fat-soluble vitamins: ADEK.
Water-soluble vitamins dissolve in water.
Water-soluble vitamins are not stored in large amounts in the body, so regular intake is needed.
Water-soluble vitamins include B group vitamins and vitamin C.
Vitamin A is important for normal vision, healthy skin and growth.
Deficiency of vitamin A causes xerophthalmia and may also cause night blindness.
Vitamin D helps in absorption of calcium and phosphorus.
Vitamin D is important for healthy bones and teeth.
Deficiency of vitamin D causes rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults.
Vitamin E helps in reproduction and acts as an antioxidant.
Deficiency of vitamin E may cause reproductive problems.
Vitamin K helps in blood clotting.
Deficiency of vitamin K causes increased bleeding time.
Vitamin B1 is called thiamine.
Deficiency of vitamin B1 causes beri-beri.
Vitamin B2 is called riboflavin.
Deficiency of vitamin B2 causes cracking of lips, inflammation of tongue and skin problems.
Vitamin B6 is called pyridoxine.
Deficiency of vitamin B6 may cause convulsions and nervous disorders.
Vitamin B12 is called cyanocobalamin.
Deficiency of vitamin B12 causes pernicious anaemia.
Vitamin C is called ascorbic acid.
Vitamin C helps in healing of wounds, healthy gums and immunity.
Deficiency of vitamin C causes scurvy, with symptoms such as bleeding gums and weakness.
Vitamin A is also called retinol and its deficiency causes xerophthalmia or night blindness.
Vitamin D is also called calciferol and its deficiency causes rickets.
Vitamin E is also called tocopherol and its deficiency may cause reproductive problems.
Vitamin K is also called phylloquinone and its deficiency causes increased bleeding time.
Most vitamins cannot be synthesised in the body and must be supplied through diet.
Vitamin D can be produced in skin by sunlight.
Vitamin K can be produced by bacteria present in the intestine.

notes.heading: Nucleic Acids: DNA and RNA
notes.text: Nucleic acids are biomolecules present in living cells which control heredity and protein synthesis. They carry genetic information from one generation to the next.
points: The two main types of nucleic acids are DNA and RNA.
DNA stands for deoxyribonucleic acid.
RNA stands for ribonucleic acid.
Nucleic acids help in storage of genetic information, transfer of hereditary characters, protein synthesis and control of cell activities.
Nucleic acids are polymers made up of repeating units called nucleotides.
Nucleic acid = polymer of nucleotides.
Each nucleotide contains a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar and a phosphate group.
Structure of nucleotide: nitrogenous base + sugar + phosphate.
Nitrogenous bases are of two types: purines and pyrimidines.
Purines have a double-ring structure.
Examples of purines: adenine (A) and guanine (G).
Pyrimidines have a single-ring structure.
Examples of pyrimidines: cytosine (C), thymine (T) and uracil (U).
DNA contains adenine, guanine, cytosine and thymine: A, G, C and T.
RNA contains adenine, guanine, cytosine and uracil: A, G, C and U.
Thymine is present in DNA, while uracil is present in RNA.
DNA contains 2-deoxyribose sugar.
RNA contains ribose sugar.
A nucleoside contains nitrogenous base and sugar but no phosphate group.
Nucleoside = base + sugar.
A nucleotide contains nitrogenous base, sugar and phosphate group.
Nucleotide = base + sugar + phosphate.
DNA has a double helical structure.
DNA consists of two polynucleotide chains twisted around each other.
The two DNA strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between nitrogenous bases.
In DNA, adenine pairs with thymine.
Base pair: A = T.
Adenine and thymine are joined by two hydrogen bonds.
In DNA, guanine pairs with cytosine.
Base pair: G triple-bond-like C.
Guanine and cytosine are joined by three hydrogen bonds.
RNA is generally single-stranded.
RNA mainly helps in protein synthesis.
In RNA, adenine pairs with uracil and guanine pairs with cytosine.
DNA contains deoxyribose sugar, thymine and is double-stranded.
RNA contains ribose sugar, uracil and is mostly single-stranded.
DNA mainly stores genetic information.
RNA mainly helps in protein synthesis.
DNA is mainly found in the nucleus.
RNA is found in the nucleus and cytoplasm.
There are three important types of RNA: mRNA, tRNA and rRNA.
mRNA stands for messenger RNA and carries genetic message from DNA to ribosome.
tRNA stands for transfer RNA and carries amino acids to ribosome during protein synthesis.
rRNA stands for ribosomal RNA and is present in ribosomes and helps in protein synthesis.

notes.heading: Hormones: Basic Idea
notes.text: Hormones are chemical substances produced by endocrine glands. They are released directly into the blood and control different body functions.
points: Hormones are also called chemical messengers.
Definition: Hormones are chemical substances produced in the body which regulate biological processes.
Hormones are produced in very small amounts.
Hormones are secreted by endocrine glands.
Hormones are carried by blood.
Hormones act on specific organs called target organs.
Hormones control growth, metabolism, reproduction and body balance.
Insulin is secreted by the pancreas and controls blood glucose level.
Glucagon is secreted by the pancreas and increases blood glucose level.
Thyroxine is secreted by the thyroid gland and controls metabolism.
Adrenaline is secreted by the adrenal gland and helps during fear, stress and emergency.
Testosterone is secreted by testes and acts as a male sex hormone.
Estrogen is secreted by ovary and acts as a female sex hormone.
Insulin reduces blood glucose level.
If insulin is not produced properly, blood glucose level increases and this may cause diabetes mellitus.
Adrenaline is called the emergency hormone.
Adrenaline is released during fear, anger, stress or danger.
Adrenaline increases heartbeat, blood pressure, breathing rate and glucose supply to muscles.
Thyroxine controls metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins and fats.
Iodine is needed for the formation of thyroxine.
Deficiency of iodine may cause goitre.
Important NCERT point: Hormones are non-nutrient chemicals which act as intercellular messengers and are produced in trace amounts.
